int f(int n) {
    if (n == 0) return 0;
    int mask = 1 << (31 - __builtin_clz(n)); // find the highest set bit. Or in C++20, bit_floor, but for older versions, need to compute.
    // Or for 32-bit numbers, find the highest bit position.
    // Alternatively, for a given n, the mask is 1 << (number of bits - 1)
    // For example, in C++, for n != 0, the highest bit can be found with:
    int highest_bit = 1 << (31 - __builtin_clz(n)); // assuming n is 32 bits and non-zero.
    int lower_part = n ^ highest_bit;
    int target_lower = highest_bit >> 1;
    int steps = f( lower_part ^ target_lower ) + 1 + f( target_lower );
    return steps;
}

So the code for the function would be:

class Solution {
public:
    int minimumOneBitOperations(int n) {
        if (n == 0) return 0;
        int mask = 1 << (31 - __builtin_clz(n));
        int lower_part = n ^ mask;
        int target_lower = mask >> 1;
        return minimumOneBitOperations(lower_part ^ target_lower) + 1 + minimumOneBitOperations(target_lower);
    }
};

int f(int n) {
    if (n == 0) return 0;
    int mask = 1 << (31 - __builtin_clz(n)); // find the highest set bit. Or in C++20, bit_floor, but for older versions, need to compute.

    int lower_part = n ^ highest_bit;
    int target_lower = highest_bit >> 1;
    int steps = f( lower_part ^ target_lower ) + 1 + f( target_lower );
    return steps;
}

class Solution {
public:
    int minimumOneBitOperations(int n) {
        if (n == 0) return 0;
        int mask = 1 << (31 - __builtin_clz(n));
        int lower_part = n ^ mask;
        int target_lower = mask >> 1;
        return minimumOneBitOperations(lower_part ^ target_lower) + 1 + minimumOneBitOperations(target_lower);
    }
};
